replication_study.hypothesis: Participants reminded of their own death (mortality salience = 'death') will give a smaller proportion of utilitarian (moral_acceptability) responses than participants reminded of pain (salience = 'pain') on conflict moral scenarios; specifically, there will be a significant interaction between salience (between-subjects: death vs pain) and moral scenario version (within-subjects: the two moral items) such that utilitarian responding decreases under death for conflict scenarios.
replication_study.study_type: Observational (generalizability test of an original Experimental claim using a cross-sectional online dataset collected to test the original experimental effect)

data_plan.source_type: Online survey (Qualtrics-style export / convenience sample generalizability survey)
data_plan.wave_or_subset: Single cross-sectional collection (collected Dec 2021 as indicated in replication_data file metadata); we will use the full dataset and then apply the manipulation-check filter as in the provided analysis script
data_plan.sample_size: Collected N = 131 rows (dataset shape = 131 x 39). Analysis sample will be N_participants_after_manipulation_check = the number of unique participant_uid with satisfactory_manipulation_response1 == 1 (the provided Rmd and the Python script filter on this variable). The CSV (data/original/35/input/replication_data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv) contains 131 observations; final used N will be computed during preprocessing (planned step).
data_plan.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant (with within-participant repeated observations across two moral items after reshaping to long)
data_plan.access_details: CSV and code are packaged in the input provided to the replication task at data/original/35/input/replication_data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv. No external access or credentials required for these files. (See files: data/original/35/input/replication_data/.)

qualification.explanation: This dataset contains the operational variables required to test the original claim: a between-subjects mortality salience manipulation labeled 'salience' (values: 'death' and 'pain'), two moral acceptability items (moral_accept and moral_accept1) which correspond to the within-subjects moral scenario versions, and a manipulation-check variable (satisfactory_manipulation_response1). The original experimental design manipulated mortality salience (death vs pain) and measured utilitarian responses across scenario versions (within-subject). The replication dataset allows the same high-level test (interaction salience x scenario) even though it is a different sample and collection mode. Evidence from files inspected: original experiment description and focal ANOVA are in original_paper.pdf (see Experiment 1 Results: F(1,83) = 6.89, p = .01; interaction) and the post-registration.json summarizes the original hypotheses and design (data/original/35/input/post_registration.json). The replication dataset contains the needed variables (data/original/35/input/replication_data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv) and an Rmd describing the intended analysis (data/original/35/input/replication_data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.Rmd).
qualification.similarity_to_original: Matches: (1) mortality salience manipulation variable present (CSV column 'salience' with values 'death' and 'pain') — see data/original/35/input/replication_data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv and codebook. (2) Two moral acceptability items: 'moral_accept' and 'moral_accept1' are present and correspond to the two within-subject scenario versions used in the original ANOVA (these are reshaped to long in the provided Rmd) — see Tremoliere_generalizability_score.Rmd. (3) Manipulation-check variable 'satisfactory_manipulation_response1' and participant IDs 'participant_uid' are present enabling the same exclusion rule and within-subject aggregation — see CSV and Rmd. The original experiment design and focal ANOVA are described in the paper (original_paper.pdf) and summarized in post_registration.json.
qualification.deviation_from_original: Deviations: (1) Original data were lab-collected experiments with university participants in Toulouse (Experiment 1: N=85) and Experiment 2 (N=110 after outlier removal) as described in the published paper (see Experiment 1 Method/Results in data/original/35/input/original_paper.pdf and post_registration.json). The replication dataset is an online generalizability survey (collected Dec 2021) rather than the original lab sample — see data/original/35/input/replication_data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv and Tremoliere_generalizability_score.Rmd. (2) The original study had four scenario presentations (two conflict, two control) and counterbalanced order; the replication CSV only contains two moral items (moral_accept and moral_accept1) as implemented in the provided generalizability survey (see Rmd). Thus the within-subjects item set is smaller and may not match the exact scenarios or counterbalancing details of the original lab experiment. (3) The original used a controlled experimental manipulation with random assignment in a lab; this replication is observational/generalizability sampling and may differ in sample composition, context, and experimental control — see original_paper.pdf (method) vs replication CSV metadata and Rmd.
qualification.notes: Caveats: (1) The replication dataset is a convenience/online sample; participant demographics and recruitment procedures differ from the original lab sample (see original_paper.pdf for original recruitment). (2) The CSV is pre-labeled; some column names are non-standard (e.g., 'moral_accept' and 'moral_accept1' rather than explicit scenario names) but the provided Rmd documents the intended interpretation. (3) The replication Rmd (and created Python script) filter on 'satisfactory_manipulation_response1' == 1; ensure this is the desired exclusion rule. (4) The codebook is provided as an .xlsx file in the replication_data folder (data/original/35/input/replication_data/tremoliere_codebook.xlsx) which should be consulted for exact variable descriptions if needed; openpyxl or pandas Excel reader may be required to parse it. (5) When executing, the script expects data at /app/data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv or will fall back to the bundled path; outputs will be created under /app/data to meet IO policy.

steps: 1) Place the CSV at /app/data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv (the provided script will try /app/data first and then the packaged input path).

2) Run the Python analysis script replication_data/tremoliere_generalizability_score__py.py (it reads the CSV, applies the manipulation-check filter satisfactory_manipulation_response1 == 1, reshapes the two moral items into long format, maps responses to binary utilitarian = 1 / not utilitarian = 0, and labels moral_scenario).
3) Compute descriptive statistics: participant count, counts and percentages of utilitarian responses by salience and scenario, verify manipulation-check pass rates.

4) Fit a binomial regression (GLM) with moral_acceptability_01 as outcome and C(salience)*C(moral_scenario) interaction as predictors; obtain cluster-robust standard errors clustered on participant_uid (this parallels the R mixed logistic approach in the Rmd but uses robust SEs to handle repeated measures).

5) Fit a mixed ANOVA for comparison with original analysis (within = moral_scenario, between = salience) using an appropriate package (pingouin in Python) on aggregated/long data; report F-statistics, p-values, partial eta-squared if available.

6) Report effect estimates (interaction coefficient from logistic model, odds ratios, confidence intervals), percentages by cell, and compare direction and magnitude to the original claim (expect death < pain for utilitarian responding on conflict items).
7) Save all model outputs, tables, and a short results summary to /app/data/analysis_results.txt and optional CSV/TXT outputs for replication record.
planned_method.models: Primary models: (A) mixed-effects or clustered binomial GLM: moral_acceptability_01 ~ salience * moral_scenario with participant random intercept (or binomial GLM with cluster-robust SEs on participant_uid). (B) Mixed ANOVA (repeated-measures) for the percentage of utilitarian responses per participant: within-subject factor moral_scenario (2 levels) and between-subject factor salience (death vs pain) to reproduce the original F-test logic.
planned_method.outcome_variable: moral_acceptability_01 (binary: 1 = utilitarian/moral acceptability, 0 = not moral). In aggregated/descriptive outputs we will also compute percentage utilitarian per participant across the two items if desired.
planned_method.independent_variables: Primary: salience (between-subjects: 'death' vs 'pain'), moral_scenario (within-subjects: 'impartial_beneficience' vs 'partial_beneficience' mapped from moral_accept and moral_accept1), and their interaction.
planned_method.control_variables: Planned secondary models can include demographics available in the CSV (age, gender, education, political items) as covariates if balance checks indicate differences across salience groups. Primary test will not include additional covariates to match the original ANOVA logic; order counterbalancing is not available/explicit in this dataset.
planned_method.tools_software: Python 3.10+; required packages: pandas, numpy, statsmodels, pingouin (for mixed ANOVA), scipy, openpyxl (if reading codebook), and optionally statsmodels' mixed-effects API (statsmodels), or lme4-equivalent via statsmodels. The replication script is written in Python and saved at data/original/35/input/replication_data/tremoliere_generalizability_score__py.py. R code exists (Tremoliere_generalizability_score.Rmd) but we've provided a Python translation to meet execution requirements.

planned_estimation_and_test.estimation: Estimate the interaction effect between salience and moral_scenario in a binomial model (log-odds coefficient) and report odds ratio and 95% CI; also report descriptive percentage differences (percentage points) in utilitarian responding by cell.
planned_estimation_and_test.test: Hypothesis test for interaction: z-test (Wald test) on the interaction term in the binomial GLM (cluster-robust SEs) and F-test in the mixed ANOVA for salience x scenario interaction. For pairwise comparisons of death vs pain within the conflict scenario, use Wald z-tests (logistic) or t-tests on participant-level percentages as secondary checks.

planned_method.missing_data_handling: Listwise deletion for missing outcome values. Exclude participants who fail the manipulation check (satisfactory_manipulation_response1 != 1) consistent with the provided Rmd. If entire participant data are missing for one of the moral items, handle as missing in the within-subject model (pingouin or mixed model will drop missing rows); report N used in each analysis.
planned_method.multiple_testing_policy: Primary analysis focuses on a single interaction (salience x moral_scenario). No multiplicity correction planned for the primary test. If multiple secondary outcomes or subgroup tests are performed, adjust p-values using Bonferroni or false discovery rate (Benjamini-Hochberg) as appropriate and report adjusted p-values.
planned_method.inference_criteria: Primary threshold alpha = 0.05 (two-tailed). Directional expectation (death < pain on utilitarian responding) can be highlighted, but tests will be two-tailed for robustness. A successful replication will be judged by the interaction being in the same direction as the original and statistically significant (p < 0.05) or, if not significant, by reporting effect-size and confidence intervals to assess compatibility with the original effect size.

files.replication_data/tremoliere_generalizabi: Python translation of the provided RMarkdown analysis. This script reads Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv (prefers /app/data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv), reshapes the two moral accept variables into long form, applies the manipulation-check filter (satisfactory_manipulation_response1 == 1), maps responses to binary (1 = utilitarian), computes descriptive tables, fits a binomial GLM with interaction (cluster-robust SEs clustered on participant_uid), attempts a mixed ANOVA via pingouin (if available), and writes a plain-text results summary to /app/data/analysis_results.txt. This is the main execution entrypoint for replication.
files.replication_data/Tremoliere_generalizabi: Primary dataset file containing survey responses, moral_accept and moral_accept1 items, salience condition, manipulation-check, demographics, participant_uid. Located at data/original/35/input/replication_data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv. Execution expects to find this file at /app/data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv per IO policy; the script will fall back to the packaged path if necessary.

Original RMarkdown used by the generalizability team (not executed by default). It documents an analysis pipeline in R (glmer logistic mixed model and ANOVA). Provided for reference and to mirror the logic; the Python script preserves the same analytic steps.
files.replication_data/tremoliere_codebook.xls: Codebook describing variables and item wording; consult for variable interpretation when needed. The Python script does not require the codebook to run but it is useful for human verification.

codebase.notes: Execution entrypoint: run the Python script replication_data/tremoliere_generalizability_score__py.py using Python. The script attempts to locate the CSV in /app/data first (to respect the IO policy). All outputs are written to /app/data (analysis_results.txt). The code uses only standard scientific Python packages (pandas, statsmodels, pingouin). If pingouin is unavailable, the script will still run the GLM and produce results; mixed ANOVA will be skipped with a message. The original R code remains unchanged and is preserved for human inspection; the translated Python script is intended for automated execution in the container described below.

analysis.instructions: 1) Ensure Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv is accessible at /app/data/Tremoliere_generalizability_score.csv (or leave it in the packaged path data/original/35/input/replication_data/, the script includes a fallback). 2) Install Python packages listed in docker_specs (pip install -r requirements.txt where requirements.txt lists the packages). 3) Run the script: python data/original/35/input/replication_data/tremoliere_generalizability_score__py.py. 4) The script will create /app/data/analysis_results.txt with: descriptive counts, GLM naive and cluster-robust summaries, and (if pingouin available) a mixed ANOVA table. 5) Inspect the interaction term (salience:moral_scenario) in the GLM for sign and significance and compare with the original reported interaction (original paper: F(1,83)=6.89, p=.01; see data/original/35/input/original_paper.pdf and post_registration.json).
analysis.comparison_metrics: Compare (1) direction of effect (death < pain on utilitarian responding for conflict item), (2) statistical significance (p < 0.05), (3) magnitude: percentage point difference in utilitarian responding per cell and estimated effect size (odds ratio from logistic model; partial eta-squared from ANOVA if available), and (4) confidence intervals overlap with original effect estimate. Report whether the replication effect is consistent in direction and whether the effect size is of comparable magnitude (e.g., the original reported ~20 percentage-point decrease in similar conditions; see original_paper.pdf and post_registration.json).
